section.title: Come and See What God Has Done
section.sectionRationale: A hymn calling all the earth to make a joyful noise to God, remembering the exodus deliverance through sea and river, this psalm moves from corporate praise of God's awesome deeds to personal testimony of a worshiper who was tested like refined silver, brought through fire and water, and heard when he cried out with an honest, sin-free heart.
section.thesis: The God who parted the sea for Israel's exodus, refines His people like silver through fire and water, and hears the prayer of an honest heart, calling every nation to come and see His deeds and every believer to come and hear what He has personally done for their soul.
section.frameNote: Three movements: a summons for all the earth to make a joyful noise and come see God's awesome deeds, verses 1-7; corporate praise for testing His people like silver and bringing them through fire and water into abundance, verses 8-12; and personal testimony of vows fulfilled, testing endured, and prayer heard because the heart was kept honest, verses 13-20.

units.label: Make a joyful noise to God
units.note: a summons for all the earth to see His awesome deeds

verses.text: Make a joyful noise to God, all the earth!
Sing the glory of His name; make His praise glorious.
Say to God, "How awesome are Your deeds! So great is Your power that Your enemies cower before You.
All the earth bows down to You; they sing praise to You; they sing praise to Your name." Selah
Come and see the works of God; how awesome are His deeds toward mankind.
He turned the sea into dry land; they passed through the waters on foot; there we rejoiced in Him.
He rules forever by His power; His eyes watch the nations. Do not let the rebellious exalt themselves. Selah

units.label: You have refined us like silver
units.note: tested and brought through fire and water into abundance

verses.text: Bless our God, O peoples; let the sound of His praise be heard.
He preserves our lives and keeps our feet from slipping.
For You, O God, have tested us; You have {{tsaraph|refined}} us like silver.
You led us into the net; You laid burdens on our backs.
You let men ride over our heads; we went through fire and water, but You brought us into abundance.

units.label: God has surely heard
units.note: personal testimony of vows fulfilled and prayer heard

verses.text: I will enter Your house with burnt offerings; I will fulfill my vows to You,
the vows that my lips promised and my mouth spoke in my distress.
I will offer You fatlings as burnt offerings, with the fragrant smoke of rams; I will offer bulls and goats. Selah
Come and listen, all you who fear God, and I will declare what He has done for me.
I cried out to Him with my mouth and praised Him with my tongue.
If I had cherished {{aven|iniquity}} in my heart, the Lord would not have listened.
But God has surely heard; He has attended to the sound of my prayer.
Blessed be God, who has not rejected my prayer or withheld from me His loving devotion!

tsaraph.gloss: To refine or smelt, applying intense heat to remove impurity from precious metal. God's testing of His people is pictured as this deliberate, purposeful refining, painful but productive, unlike random or purposeless suffering.

aven.gloss: Iniquity, wickedness, trouble, often connected to idolatry or deceit. The psalmist ties answered prayer directly to not cherishing this in the heart, teaching that an honestly kept heart, not sinless perfection, is what keeps the channel of prayer open.

basic.context: This psalm calls the whole earth to see God's awesome deeds, remembering how He turned the sea into dry land at the exodus. It then turns personal, a worshiper testifying that God tested him like refined silver, brought him through fire and water, and heard his prayer because he did not cherish sin in his heart.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm moves naturally from corporate praise of God's historical deeds to individual testimony, showing that the God who acted for the whole nation also personally tests and hears the individual believer.
basic.originalLanguages: God tsaraph, refines, His people like silver, verse 10, a deliberate, purposeful testing rather than random suffering. And the psalmist ties answered prayer to not cherishing aven, iniquity, in the heart, verse 18, an honestly kept heart rather than sinless perfection.
basic.typology: God turning the sea into dry land so His people passed through on foot, verse 6, recalls the exodus and anticipates the greater deliverance through Christ, who leads His people through death itself into resurrection life, just as Israel passed through the sea into freedom.
basic.christ: Christ is the one through whom God's people are truly brought 'through fire and water... into abundance,' verse 12, His own death and resurrection the pattern and power by which every trial of His people finally issues not in destruction but in overflowing life, Romans 8:37.
basic.god: God is shown as the sovereign ruler of every nation whose historic deeds, like parting the sea, call for universal praise, and as the personal refiner of His people, testing them like silver, yet also the one who hears and answers the honest prayer of a kept heart.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 14:21-22 records God turning the sea into dry land, the historic deed this psalm recalls. 1 Peter 1:6-7 compares trials to the refining of gold by fire, echoing this psalm's image of testing like silver. James 5:16 teaches that the prayer of a righteous person is powerful and effective, echoing this psalm's link between an honest heart and answered prayer.
basic.oneStory: The exodus deliverance through the sea and the personal testing of the psalmist through fire and water both anticipate the greater deliverance accomplished in Christ, who leads His people through every trial, even death itself, into the abundance of resurrection life.

context.literary: A hymn moving from universal summons to praise, verses 1-7, through corporate testimony of God's refining and preserving His people, verses 8-12, to intensely personal testimony of vows and answered prayer, verses 13-20, narrowing from all the earth to one worshiper's own heart.
context.historical: Its explicit memory of God turning the sea into dry land, verse 6, ties the psalm to Israel's exodus deliverance, Exodus 14:21-22, likely sung at a festival recalling that founding act of national salvation alongside more recent, unspecified deliverances.
context.canonical: Its structure, moving from the community's testimony to the individual's own, models the pattern found throughout the Psalter of grounding personal faith in the larger, remembered story of God's historical acts for His whole people.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To summon all the earth to praise God for His historic, awesome deeds, and to testify personally that the same God who acted for the nation also tests, preserves, and hears the individual who keeps an honest heart before Him.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific historical deliverance, the exodus through the sea, and a particular worshiper's testimony of vows and answered prayer; it prescribes as an abiding pattern grounding personal faith in God's remembered, corporate acts and keeping an honest heart as the condition for confident prayer.

debates.question: Does verse 18, 'If I had cherished iniquity in my heart, the Lord would not have listened,' teach that any unconfessed sin blocks all prayer, or something narrower?

views.label: The verse describes a general principle that persistent, unrepented, cherished sin, not mere imperfection, hinders a person's prayers from being heard.
views.case: The word 'cherished' implies deliberate holding onto and valuing sin, not the ordinary, confessed imperfection every believer carries, distinguishing this from a claim of sinless perfection.

views.label: The verse specifically addresses the psalmist's own personal integrity in this particular crisis, testifying that his prayer in this instance was not compromised by hidden hypocrisy, rather than laying down a universal rule for all prayer.
views.case: The verse is testimony ('if I had... the Lord would not have'), a specific claim about this occasion of answered prayer, rather than a general doctrinal statement meant to be applied rigidly to every prayer request.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the verse teaches that genuine, honest-hearted prayer, not the harboring of cherished, unrepented sin, is what God hears, a pattern consistent with the rest of Scripture's teaching on approaching God with a clean conscience, 1 John 1:9.

study.originalLanguages: God tsaraph, refines, His people like silver, verse 10, a deliberate, purposeful process rather than random suffering. And the psalmist ties his answered prayer to not cherishing aven, iniquity, in his heart, verse 18, an honestly kept heart rather than a claim to sinless perfection.

typology.type: God turning the sea into dry land so His people passed through on foot, verse 6
typology.fulfillment: Recalls the exodus and anticipates the greater deliverance through Christ, who leads His people through death itself into resurrection life.

typology.type: Being brought 'through fire and water... into abundance,' verse 12
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, whose own death and resurrection is the pattern and power by which every trial of His people issues not in destruction but in overflowing life, Romans 8:37.

study.christ: Christ is the one through whom God's people are truly brought 'through fire and water... into abundance,' verse 12, His own death and resurrection the pattern and power by which every trial of His people finally issues not in destruction but in overflowing life, Romans 8:37. And the exodus deliverance this psalm remembers, God turning the sea into dry land, verse 6, previews the greater exodus Christ accomplishes, leading His people through death itself into resurrection freedom, Luke 9:31.
study.god: God is shown as the sovereign ruler of every nation whose historic deeds, like parting the sea, call for universal praise, and as the personal refiner of His people, testing them like silver, yet also the one who hears and answers the honest prayer of a kept heart.

crossReferences.note: Records God turning the sea into dry land, the historic deliverance this psalm recalls in verse 6.
Compares trials to the refining of gold by fire, echoing this psalm's image of testing like silver, verse 10.
Teaches that the prayer of a righteous person is powerful and effective, echoing this psalm's link between an honest heart and answered prayer.

study.oneStory: The exodus deliverance through the sea and the personal testing of the psalmist through fire and water both anticipate the greater deliverance accomplished in Christ, who leads His people through every trial, even death itself, into the abundance of resurrection life.

quiz.q: What historical event does the psalm recall in verse 6?

options.text: God turning the sea into dry land so His people passed through on foot.
options.note: This recalls the exodus deliverance through the Red Sea, Exodus 14:21-22.

options.text: The building of Solomon's temple.
options.note: The verse describes a sea crossing, not a temple's construction.

options.text: The giving of the law at Sinai.
options.note: The verse specifically recalls passing through the sea, not the giving of the law.

options.text: The conquest of Jericho's walls.
options.note: The verse describes a sea crossing, a different event from the fall of Jericho.

quiz.q: How does the psalm describe God's testing of His people, in verse 10?

options.text: You have refined us like silver.
options.note: This pictures deliberate, purposeful testing that removes impurity, like the refining of precious metal.

options.text: You have abandoned us to random chance.
options.note: The image is purposeful refining, not abandonment to chance.

options.text: You have hidden us from every trial.
options.note: The psalm describes real testing endured, not the avoidance of all trial.

options.text: You have punished us without any purpose.
options.note: The refining image specifically implies purpose, removing impurity, not purposeless punishment.

quiz.q: What does verse 12 say is the outcome of going 'through fire and water'?

options.text: God brought them into abundance.
options.note: The trial ends not in destruction but in overflowing provision.

options.text: They were left permanently in hardship.
options.note: The verse explicitly names abundance as the outcome, not ongoing hardship.

options.text: They lost everything they once had.
options.note: The verse describes gain, abundance, as the trial's outcome, not loss.

options.text: They were scattered among the nations.
options.note: The specific outcome named is abundance, not scattering.

quiz.q: What condition does the psalmist name for God hearing his prayer, in verse 18?

options.text: Not cherishing iniquity in his heart.
options.note: The psalmist ties answered prayer to an honestly kept heart, not sinless perfection.

options.text: Offering the largest possible sacrifice.
options.note: While sacrifices are mentioned earlier, verse 18 specifically names the heart's honesty as the condition.

options.text: Being born into a priestly family.
options.note: No such requirement is mentioned; the condition named is the heart's honesty.

options.text: Praying only in the temple in Jerusalem.
options.note: The condition given is the heart's integrity, not the specific location of prayer.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's picture of deliverance through the sea and through fire and water connect to Christ?

options.text: Christ accomplishes the greater exodus, leading His people through death itself into resurrection life.
options.note: This fulfills the pattern of deliverance through trial into abundance that both the exodus and the psalmist's testimony display.

options.text: Christ has no connection to deliverance through trial in the New Testament.
options.note: Romans 8:37 and Luke 9:31 both connect Christ directly to deliverance through trial into greater life.

options.text: Christ promises His followers will never face any fire or water, any trial at all.
options.note: The New Testament promises deliverance through trials, not the absence of all trials, 1 Peter 1:6-7.

options.text: Christ's death is entirely unrelated to the theme of testing or refining.
options.note: 1 Peter 1:6-7 explicitly connects the refining of faith through trials to the pattern fulfilled in Christ.

questions: The psalm calls 'all the earth' to make a joyful noise and come see what God has done, verses 1 and 5. What specific thing has God done in your life recently that is worth telling someone else about this week?

God refines His people like silver, verse 10, a purposeful process rather than random suffering. How does viewing your current difficulty as purposeful refining, rather than meaningless hardship, change how you endure it?

The psalm testifies that trials end not in destruction but in abundance, 'You brought us into abundance,' verse 12. Where do you need to trust that your current fire and water will lead somewhere good, even if you cannot see it yet?

The psalmist ties answered prayer to not cherishing sin in his heart, verse 18. Is there anything you are currently holding onto, rather than confessing honestly to God, that may be affecting your own prayer life?

The psalm moves from corporate praise, 'all the earth,' to deeply personal testimony, 'I will declare what He has done for me,' verse 16. What is your own personal testimony of what God has done that you could share with someone this week?

translationNotes.note: Refined renders tsaraph, the smelting process that removes impurity from precious metal through intense heat, a deliberate image of purposeful, productive testing.
Praised Him with my tongue can also be rendered and His praise was on my tongue, either reading emphasizing that praise accompanied the cry for help rather than following only after deliverance.
Iniquity renders aven, often tied elsewhere to idolatry or deceit; cherished implies deliberate holding onto sin rather than the ordinary imperfection every believer carries and confesses.

meta.notes: BSB text retrieved via bible.com (YouVersion, Berean Standard Bible, version 3034) after the local makor-scripture toolkit failed with a persistent disk I/O error reading the sandbox-mounted database. Verse numbering and count (20 verses) verified against Psalm 66:1-66:20 per PSALMS-SECTION-MAP.md. The musical superscription is not rendered as verse text, consistent with the established convention in this corpus. A minor spacing artifact in the source scrape of verse 10 ('For You , O God') was corrected to standard spacing. No em or en dashes present (one was found in the raw fetched source at verse 14 and converted to a comma). No sermon link could be verified this session from the trusted cluster for Psalm 66 specifically, so sermon status is none.
